Jesus sends out seventy-two workers into a harvest that is both plentiful and perilous. They are to go as lambs among wolves—vulnerable, dependent, stripped of the security blankets we typically cling to. No purse, no bag, no sandals. The point is stark: your confidence cannot rest in provision you've secured for yourself. You must trust that the Master of the harvest will provide through the very people you serve. This is not sentimentality about faith; it is radical exposure. And Jesus makes clear that rejection will come. When it does, you are not to linger, negotiating or hoping for a better outcome. You shake the dust off and move on, declaring that the kingdom has come near regardless. The seventy-two return exhilarated by the authority they wielded—demons submitted to them in Jesus' name. But Jesus reorients their joy. Yes, He has given them power over the enemy, but this is not what should move their hearts. What should move them is that their names are written in heaven. The most dangerous spiritual mistake is to become fascinated with the supernatural machinery of God's kingdom rather than grateful for salvation itself.

In that moment, Jesus rejoices in the Holy Spirit and prays to His Father with a declaration that cuts against all expectation: God has hidden these truths from the wise and learned and revealed them to little children. The sophisticated have disqualified themselves through their confidence in their own understanding. The simple receive what they cannot earn or deduce. Jesus then speaks a word that should make us tremble with longing: prophets and kings desired to see what these disciples see and did not. We are living in the fulfillment of the ages. We have access to the revelation of the Son that surpasses what the faithful of old could only anticipate. Yet how casually we treat it. How often we take for granted what they died longing to see.

An expert in the law tries to trap Jesus with a question about eternal life, but the trap springs backward. When the man correctly recites that he must love God and neighbor with everything he has, Jesus tells him: do this and you will live. The chasm opens immediately. The man wants to justify himself—wants to narrow the definition of neighbor so that he can claim he has kept this impossible standard. Jesus answers with the parable of the Good Samaritan, a story that does not comfort the lawyer's conscience but shatters it. A Samaritan, of all people—an outsider, an enemy—becomes the model of neighborly compassion. The man asked who his neighbor is. Jesus shows him that being a neighbor is not a category to master but a posture of mercy to practice. Then He tells the lawyer to go and do likewise. The demand is the same as before: love as yourself, without qualification, without the luxury of exceptions. Jesus does not soften the law; He deepens it into an abyss the comfortable cannot cross on their own strength.

In the final scene, Martha busies herself with hospitality while Mary sits at Jesus' feet listening. Martha's complaint seems reasonable—shouldn't her sister help with the work? But Jesus gently corrects her. Martha is worried and upset about many things; Mary has chosen the good portion, and it will not be taken away from her. The work matters, but not more than intimacy with Jesus Himself. The sitting, the listening, the undivided attention—this is what remains. This is what has eternal weight. We pour ourselves into doing good works, managing our relationships, securing our futures, solving our problems, only to discover that we have missed the one thing necessary. Mary understood something Martha had to learn: His presence is the treasure, and sitting with Him is the only thing that cannot be lost.
